lessons.title: Why Write Anything Down?
lessons.content:
Picture your favourite home-cooked meal. If the only person who knows how to make it never writes the recipe down, the dish disappears the day they forget it or are not around. Now write that recipe on a card, and anyone can recreate the meal, any time. That card is **documentation**: knowledge written down so it can be used again.

**Documentation** simply means recording information about something so others (and future-you) can build it, use it, or fix it without guessing. Flat-pack furniture is a great example: the assembly instructions let a total stranger build the shelf, even though they never met the designer.

Why bother? Because knowledge that lives only in one person's head is **fragile**. People forget details, get busy, or move on. When the know-how is only in a head, it can vanish. Written down, it becomes repeatable (anyone can follow it), shareable (you can hand it to someone else), and survivable (it outlasts any one person).

There is a handy way to think about it: **documentation is a letter to your future self**. Six months from now you will not remember why you did something or how it worked. A few clear notes save your future self hours of re-figuring-it-out.

So the first habit is simple: when something matters, write it down clearly. The rest of this sub-topic is about *what* to write and *how* to make it genuinely useful.

items.prompt: What does 'documentation' mean, in plain terms?
choices: Optional busywork you do only if there is spare time at the end
A drawing of how the finished product should look
The list of people who worked on a project
Recording information so others, and future-you, can build, use, or fix something
items.explanation: Documentation is knowledge written down so it can be reused, like a recipe or assembly instructions. Treating it as optional busywork is the classic trap; documentation is what makes work repeatable. A drawing or a list of people is not the same as recorded know-how for building, using, or fixing something.

items.prompt: Why is knowledge that lives only in one person's head described as 'fragile'?
choices: Because that person is probably not very smart
Because heads can only hold a small amount of information
Because people forget, get busy, or move on, and then the knowledge can vanish
Because spoken knowledge is always wrong
items.explanation: Head-only knowledge is fragile because the single person can forget details, be unavailable, or leave, taking the know-how with them. It has nothing to do with how smart they are or how much a head can hold, and spoken knowledge is not automatically wrong. Writing it down is what makes it survive.

items.prompt: A friend writes down their family recipe so anyone can recreate the dish later. Which benefit of documentation does this best show?
choices: It makes the knowledge repeatable and shareable, not stuck in one head
It proves the recipe is the tastiest in the world
It guarantees nobody else will ever cook that dish
It means the recipe never needs to be updated
items.explanation: Writing the recipe down lets anyone follow it (repeatable) and lets it be handed to others (shareable), so it no longer depends on one person's memory. It does not prove the dish is the best, it does not lock others out, and recipes can still need updating. The point is reuse beyond the original cook.

items.prompt: What does the phrase 'documentation is a letter to your future self' mean?
choices: You should mail your notes to yourself in the post
Notes are only ever useful to the person who wrote them
Clear notes now save future-you from re-figuring-out what you already knew
You should write documentation only after you have forgotten everything
items.explanation: The phrase means that months later you will have forgotten the details, so clear notes written now help your future self pick the work back up quickly. It is not about literal mail, and it is a misconception that docs are only for the writer; others read them too. You write while you still remember, not after forgetting.

items.prompt: Flat-pack furniture comes with assembly instructions. Why are those instructions a good example of documentation?
choices: They make the furniture more expensive to produce
They let a stranger who never met the designer build the furniture correctly
They are only useful to the person who designed the furniture
They replace the need for any physical parts
items.explanation: Good documentation lets someone with no prior knowledge carry out the work, exactly what assembly instructions do for a stranger building the shelf. They are written for the user, not just the designer, they do not raise production cost in any meaningful sense, and they certainly do not replace the parts. The value is transferring know-how to whoever needs it.

items.prompt: Someone says, 'I never write anything down; I just write docs for myself anyway, and I remember everything.' What is the best response?
choices: They are right; if you remember it, no one else needs it written down
They are right; writing things down is busywork that wastes time
Memory is always more reliable than any written note
Docs are mainly for others too, so written knowledge survives and can be shared, not just remembered
items.explanation: Documentation is not only for the author; it lets others build and maintain the work and survives even if you forget or leave. Believing docs are just for yourself, or that they are busywork, are the misconceptions here. Human memory is not more reliable than a clear written record, especially over time.

items.prompt: Which situation shows the value of writing things down most clearly?
choices: A team relies on one person who is the only one who knows how the system works
A new helper builds a bookshelf perfectly using the printed instructions, never having seen one before
Everyone keeps the steps only in their memory to save paper
A recipe is deliberately kept secret so the dish cannot be recreated
items.explanation: The new helper succeeding from written instructions shows documentation making work repeatable and shareable. Relying on one person, keeping steps only in memory, and hiding the recipe all do the opposite, leaving the knowledge fragile or unusable by others. Written-down know-how is what lets a newcomer act without guessing.

items.prompt: Good documentation makes work 'repeatable, shareable, and survivable.' What does 'survivable' mean here?
choices: The knowledge outlasts any one person, so it is not lost if someone leaves or forgets
The product can survive being dropped
The documentation can never be edited again
Only the original author can ever use it safely
items.explanation: Survivable means the know-how lives on beyond any single individual, so the work is not lost when a person forgets or moves on. It is about the knowledge, not the product's physical toughness. Documentation can still be edited, and it is meant for many readers, not just the author.

items.prompt: You finish a tricky task today. Why might you jot down a few clear notes even if no one else is involved?
choices: Because future-you will likely forget the details and the notes save re-figuring it out
Because notes make the task automatically run faster next time without you
Because writing notes is required before any task can be called finished
Because notes are only worth writing when a whole team will read them
items.explanation: Even solo, documentation is a letter to your future self: months later you will not recall the details, and notes spare you the rework. Notes do not magically run the task for you, they are not a universal rule for 'finished', and they are valuable even for an audience of one (your future self).

lessons.title: Specs and the Different Kinds of Docs
lessons.content:
You already know a **requirement** is a clear, testable statement of what is needed, like "a sturdy table." But "sturdy" alone does not tell a builder the exact size, materials, or how much weight it must hold. That precise, agreed description is a **specification**, or **spec** for short.

A **spec** is a precise description of *what* something is and does, with its agreed details: dimensions, behaviour, limits, and **interfaces** (the points where it connects to other things). It is the contract everyone builds and checks against. So requirements are the *needs*; the spec is the agreed, detailed description that satisfies them. For the table, the requirement is "sturdy"; the spec is "120 by 75 cm, oak top, four steel legs, holds 80 kg."

A spec is just one kind of document. Others include a **design doc**, which explains *how* something works and *why* choices were made; **user docs** (a manual or guide) that tell people *how to use* it; and a **maintenance** (or operations) doc that explains how to *fix or run* it. Two short ones are common too: a **README**, the quick "start here" overview, and a **change log**, a list of what changed and when.

The big idea is **audience-appropriate** writing: write for whoever reads it. A user manual speaks to a customer in plain steps; an internal design note speaks to a builder about trade-offs. Mixing them up, like handing a customer your internal design notes, leaves the reader lost. Match the document to its reader and its job.

items.prompt: What is a 'specification' (spec)?
choices: A rough wish for what someone hopes to get
A precise, agreed description of what something is and does, with its details and limits
A list of everyone who will use the product
A guess about how long the work will take
items.explanation: A spec is the precise, agreed description, dimensions, behaviour, limits, and interfaces, that everyone builds and checks against. A rough wish is closer to a vague requirement, not a spec. A user list and a time guess are different things entirely; neither pins down what the thing must be.

items.prompt: How do a requirement and a spec differ?
choices: They are exactly the same thing with two names
A requirement is detailed; a spec is always vague
A spec comes first, then you write the requirement from it
A requirement is the need; a spec is the agreed detailed description that satisfies it
items.explanation: Requirements state the needs (for example, 'a sturdy table'), while the spec gives the agreed precise details (exact size, materials, load) that meet those needs. They are not identical, the spec is the detailed one (not the vague one), and needs normally come before the detailed description, not after.

items.prompt: For a table, which of these is the SPEC rather than the requirement?
choices: "We need a sturdy table"
"It should feel nice and look good"
"120 by 75 cm, oak top, four steel legs, holds 80 kg"
"Something to put our dinner plates on"
items.explanation: The exact measurements, materials, and load limit are the agreed precise description, which is the spec. 'Sturdy', 'feel nice', and 'something for plates' are needs or wishes, the requirement side, not the detailed contract a builder works from.

items.prompt: Why is a spec sometimes called 'the contract everyone builds and checks against'?
choices: Because it is a legal document signed by lawyers
Because it sets the agreed details, so builders make to it and reviewers check the result against it
Because it lists the price each person must pay
Because it can be changed by anyone at any time without telling others
items.explanation: A spec works like a contract: it states the agreed details, so the people building aim for them and the people checking compare the result to them. It need not be a lawyer-signed document, it is not a price list, and a shared agreement is not something one person quietly rewrites at will.

items.prompt: Which document's job is to explain HOW something works and WHY the choices were made?
choices: A user manual
A change log
A design doc
A README
items.explanation: A design doc captures how the thing works and the reasoning behind decisions, aimed at people building or maintaining it. A user manual explains how to use it, a change log records what changed and when, and a README is the short 'start here' overview; none of those focus on the how-and-why of the design.

items.prompt: A customer just wants to know how to set up and use a product they bought. Which document is written for them?
choices: The internal design doc full of trade-off reasoning
The change log listing internal edits by date
The maintenance doc for repair technicians
The user manual (a guide in plain steps for using it)
items.explanation: User docs, like a manual, are written for the person using the product, in plain how-to steps. The design doc targets builders, the change log targets people tracking edits, and the maintenance doc targets repairers; handing a customer any of those would leave them lost. Match the doc to its reader.

items.prompt: What is a README, in plain terms?
choices: A detailed legal contract for the whole project
The quick 'start here' overview that orients a newcomer
A full repair manual for technicians
A list of everyone who paid for the project
items.explanation: A README is the short overview that tells a newcomer what something is and how to get started, the 'start here' note. It is not a legal contract, not a deep repair manual, and not a payer list. Its whole point is a fast, friendly orientation before the heavier docs.

items.prompt: What does it mean to write documentation that is 'audience-appropriate'?
choices: Write everything in the most technical language possible so it looks expert
Write one identical document and give it to absolutely everyone
Write for whoever will read it, so a user manual and an internal design note differ in style and detail
Write only for yourself, since you understand it best
items.explanation: Audience-appropriate means tailoring the document to its reader: a customer needs plain steps, a builder needs trade-off detail. Forcing maximum jargon, using one identical doc for everyone, or writing only for yourself all ignore who actually needs to act on it. The reader determines the right level and tone.

items.prompt: A change log and a maintenance doc are both useful. What does the change log specifically record?
choices: Step-by-step instructions for first-time users
What changed and when, over time
The precise agreed dimensions and materials
The reasons behind the original design choices
items.explanation: A change log lists what changed and when, giving a running history of updates. First-time use steps belong in the user manual, agreed dimensions and materials belong in the spec, and the reasoning behind choices belongs in the design doc. Each document carries a different, specific kind of information.

lessons.title: What Makes Docs Good (and What Bad Docs Cost)
lessons.content:
Having a document is not enough; it has to be a *good* one. Good documentation shares a few qualities. It is **clear and unambiguous** (only one sensible reading), **audience-appropriate** (pitched at whoever reads it), **accurate and up to date**, **findable** (organised so people can locate it), and **complete enough to act on without guessing**. Diagrams, examples, and defined terms all help.

The most dangerous failure is a **stale** doc, one that is out of date. A manual that describes last year's model can tell you to press the red button when the real product now uses a green one, causing a mistake. That is why people say a stale doc is **worse than none**: no doc makes you check carefully, but a wrong doc confidently misleads you.

The fix is a **single source of truth**: one authoritative copy that everyone refers to, instead of five conflicting drafts emailed around. We have all seen the chaos of "final_v2_FINAL_really.doc" where nobody knows which copy is correct. Because documents change, we also track **versions**, so it is clear which is the latest and what changed.

Why care so much? The **cost of bad or missing docs** is real: wasted time re-figuring things out, mistakes from misunderstanding, and the **bus factor**, the risk that if the one person who knows everything is suddenly gone, the work grinds to a halt. To wrap up: specs say precisely what to build; documentation makes it usable and maintainable; and good docs are clear, current, audience-aware, and kept as a single source of truth.

items.prompt: Which set of qualities best describes GOOD documentation?
choices: Long, full of jargon, and impressive-looking
Clear, audience-appropriate, accurate and up to date, findable, and complete enough to act on
Written once and never touched again
Kept private so only the author can read it
items.explanation: Good docs are clear, pitched to their reader, current, easy to find, and complete enough to use without guessing. Length and jargon do not make docs good, never updating them invites staleness, and keeping them private defeats the point of sharing knowledge.

items.prompt: Why is a stale (out-of-date) document often called 'worse than no document at all'?
choices: Because it takes up storage space
Because old documents are always longer than new ones
Because it can only be read by the original author
Because it confidently misleads you into doing the wrong thing, while no doc makes you check carefully
items.explanation: A stale doc looks trustworthy but describes something that is no longer true, so it actively misleads, whereas having no doc at least prompts you to verify. The danger is being confidently wrong, not storage space or length, and readership is not the issue. Out-of-date guidance causes mistakes precisely because people trust it.

items.prompt: A manual says 'press the red button to stop', but the actual product now has a green stop button. What problem is this?
choices: The manual is too short
The manual is stale: it no longer matches the real product, so it can cause a mistake
The manual is using the wrong language
There is nothing wrong; manuals never need to match the product
items.explanation: This is a stale doc: it describes an older version, so a reader following it presses the wrong control and errs. Length and language are not the issue, and it is a misconception that a manual need not match the product; an accurate, up-to-date manual is exactly what prevents this mistake.

items.prompt: What is a 'single source of truth' for documentation?
choices: The oldest copy of the document, kept for history
A copy stored on every person's own computer
One authoritative copy that everyone refers to, instead of many conflicting copies
A document that no one is allowed to change
items.explanation: A single source of truth is the one official copy everyone uses, so there is no confusion about which version is correct. The oldest copy is not authoritative, scattering copies onto everyone's computer creates the very conflict you want to avoid, and the source of truth is updated over time, not frozen.

items.prompt: A team emails around files named 'plan_final.doc', 'plan_final_v2.doc', and 'plan_v2_FINAL_really.doc'. Which idea fixes this mess?
choices: Keep even more copies, since more backups are always safer
Keep one shared, versioned document as the single source of truth
Delete all documentation so there is nothing to conflict
Let each person trust whichever copy they happen to have
items.explanation: One shared, versioned copy that everyone refers to ends the confusion over which file is correct. Keeping more conflicting copies makes it worse, not safer; deleting all docs throws away needed knowledge; and trusting whatever copy you hold is exactly how mistakes creep in. The cure is a single source of truth with tracked versions.

items.prompt: Why do we track 'versions' of a document?
choices: To make the document look more official
Because documents change, so versions show which copy is the latest and what changed
To stop anyone from ever editing the document
Because every reader needs their own private edition
items.explanation: Versioning records that documents evolve, making it clear which copy is current and what was changed. It is not about appearances, it does not freeze the document (it embraces change in an orderly way), and the goal is one shared history, not a private edition per reader. Versions keep a changing doc trustworthy.

items.prompt: What does the 'bus factor' describe?
choices: How many people can fit on a project team
How fast a project can travel from start to finish
The cost of commuting to the office
The risk that if the one person who knows everything is suddenly gone, the work grinds to a halt
items.explanation: Bus factor is the danger of depending on a single person whose knowledge is not written down; if they leave or are unavailable, progress stops. It is not about team size, project speed, or commuting. Documentation lowers the bus factor by spreading knowledge so no single absence is fatal.

items.prompt: You inherit a project with no notes, no README, and no spec. What is the most likely cost of these missing docs?
choices: The project will automatically run faster without paperwork
Wasted time re-figuring things out and mistakes from misunderstanding
Nothing; missing docs never cause problems
The project becomes cheaper because no one wrote anything
items.explanation: Missing documentation forces you to reconstruct knowledge from scratch and invites errors from guessing, the real cost of bad or no docs. It does not speed things up or save money in any genuine sense, and the idea that missing docs are harmless is the misconception this lesson warns against. Inheriting a no-notes project is a classic painful example.

items.prompt: Pulling it together: which statement best summarises the role of specs and documentation?
choices: Specs and docs are optional extras you add only if a customer demands them
A spec and a user manual are the same document with different covers
Specs say precisely what to build; documentation makes it usable and maintainable, and good docs stay clear, current, audience-aware, and a single source of truth
Keeping several conflicting copies is safer than maintaining one authoritative version
items.explanation: The spec pins down precisely what to build, while documentation (design, user, maintenance, README, change log) makes it usable and maintainable, and good docs are clear, current, audience-appropriate, and kept as one source of truth. Treating docs as optional, confusing a spec with a user manual, and preferring many conflicting copies are all misconceptions the sub-topic set out to correct.
